common_name: Super Sweet 100 Cherry Tomato (Red Cherry Cluster Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Super Sweet 100 is a vigorous indeterminate that needs very sturdy staking or caging to at least 6 feet.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Super Sweet 100 is one of the most prolific and most frequently recommended cherry tomato varieties in American home gardening — a hybrid producing massive, long clusters (trusses) of 20–30 small (1-inch), round, brilliant red cherry tomatoes with exceptional sweetness and high sugar content that makes each fruit taste like concentrated tomato candy. The 'Sweet 100' line was introduced in the 1970s and immediately became one of the best-selling tomato varieties; 'Super Sweet 100' is an improved hybrid with better disease resistance. In zone 9, Super Sweet 100 produces from February or March transplanting through June, and again from September through November transplanting for a fall crop — two productive growing windows that can yield hundreds of fruits per plant. The enormous, arching clusters of brilliant red tomatoes draping from a well-established vine are a spectacular sight. The high Brix (sugar content) makes Super Sweet 100 outstanding for snacking, caprese salads, and any use where raw tomato sweetness is the primary quality desired. Vigorous growth requires staking or caging — an established Super Sweet 100 vine can reach 8 feet or more and must be supported to prevent collapsing under the weight of developing fruit clusters.

common_name: Black Cherry Tomato (Dark Purple-Red Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Black Cherry is a true open-pollinated heirloom with seed that can be saved true-to-type.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Black Cherry is one of the most unusual and striking cherry tomato varieties — an heirloom producing small (1-inch), round fruits in a deep mahogany-purple to almost black color with brick-red flesh, offering a distinctly rich, complex, wine-like flavor with chocolate-tomato undertones that is remarkably different from standard red or yellow cherry tomatoes. The dark color comes from high anthocyanin content in the skin — the same antioxidant pigments responsible for the dark color of blueberries and black raspberries — giving Black Cherry a distinctive nutritional profile alongside its unusual flavor. The complex, slightly smoky, wine-tomato flavor makes Black Cherry outstanding in raw preparations where the flavor stands alone: fresh from the vine, in caprese-style salads with mozzarella and basil, or in mixed cherry tomato platters where its dark color contrasts dramatically with red Sun Gold and yellow pear tomatoes. The indeterminate vine produces prolific clusters from transplanting through the end of the warm season. As an open-pollinated heirloom, true seed can be saved from season to season. Resistance to cracking (a weakness of many dark-colored tomatoes) is better than many purple/black tomato varieties.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Prairie Fire develops its best red summer foliage color in full sun — in partial shade the foliage remains more green through summer before turning red in fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Prairie Fire is one of the most distinctive switchgrass cultivars — a compact selection that develops an early and sustained red-to-burgundy foliage color through the summer growing season, rather than the typical green-turning-to-red-in-fall pattern of most switchgrass cultivars. In full sun from July onward, Prairie Fire's foliage transitions progressively from green to red-tipped to fully wine-red — providing a strong red color contribution throughout the second half of the growing season when most other ornamental grasses are still green. The compact habit (3–4 feet tall, compared to 5–6 feet for species switchgrass) makes it proportionate for typical residential garden beds and mixed perennial borders. Like all Panicum virgatum cultivars, Prairie Fire is a North American prairie native that is exceptionally adaptable: tolerating clay, drought, wet conditions, and the full range of Gulf South weather extremes. The early red coloration makes it excellent for mixed perennial borders where the early fall-like color creates a dramatic seasonal transition effect while summer still has weeks to run. Plant in masses for maximum visual impact — the red-burgundy foliage color is most effective when multiple plants create a sweeping color echo across a broad planting area.

common_name: Cloud Nine Dogwood (Heavy-Blooming White Flowering Dogwood)

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container trees in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; Cloud Nine is particularly valued for its prolific blooming even at an early age — often flowering in the first or second year after planting, earlier than many dogwood cultivars.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; Cloud Nine is one of the most widely admired white flowering dogwood cultivars — selected for its unusually profuse bloom (the bracts cover the tree so densely at peak bloom that the branches are nearly invisible), its somewhat horizontally spreading branch structure that creates a wider than tall profile, and its ability to bloom as a young tree in its first or second year rather than requiring 5–7 years before flowering as is common with seedling-grown trees. The large, overlapping, pure-white bracts (each slightly overlapping the adjacent bract, creating a solid canopy of white) cover the entire tree at peak bloom in March through April in zone 9 — the flowering display has been described as looking like the tree is covered in white clouds, giving the cultivar its name. The wider-than-tall spreading form gives Cloud Nine a distinctive silhouette even when not in flower — an elegant horizontal branching pattern that is attractive year-round. Excellent red to maroon fall foliage color and red berries for wildlife follow the spring bloom. In zone 9, plant in morning sun and afternoon shade on the north or east side of structures to minimize summer heat stress on this understory native.

planting.note: Plant container trees in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.0) soil in partial shade; Venus is more heat-tolerant and disease-resistant than Cornus florida and performs better in zone 9 than the native species.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Venus is the US National Arboretum's most celebrated hybrid dogwood introduction — a first-generation hybrid between the native American flowering dogwood (Cornus florida) and the Chinese kousa dogwood (C. kousa) that produces flower bracts of extraordinary size: individual bracts (modified leaves surrounding the true flowers) reach 3–4 inches long, making the overall flower display twice the visual impact of either parent species. The enormous, pure-white, overlapping bracts create a spectacular late-April to May floral display (blooming slightly later than C. florida, extending the dogwood season) on a vigorous, upright tree. Venus inherits the best qualities of both parents: the early spring bloom timing and dense flowering of C. florida, combined with the superior heat tolerance, drought resistance, and disease resistance of C. kousa — specifically resistance to dogwood anthracnose that has devastated native C. florida populations. In zone 9, Venus performs reliably where native dogwood often struggles — the C. kousa genetics provide significantly better adaptation to Gulf South heat, humidity, and the summer conditions that stress C. florida. The large fruits that follow bloom are more showy than those of either parent. A landmark introduction for southern gardeners who love dogwoods.
